equations.name: Newton's Second Law
equations.use: Predicts how an object accelerates under a net force. Doubling F doubles a; doubling m halves a.

equations.name: Net acceleration at liftoff

variables.meaning: Gravitational acceleration (9.8 m/s² on Earth's surface)

equations.use: Tells you whether a rocket lifts off and how fast it accelerates. T must exceed m·g for liftoff. Doubling thrust OR halving mass both double acceleration.

equations.name: Tsiolkovsky rocket equation
equations.use: Limits how much delta-v you get from a fixed fuel mass. The 'tyranny of the rocket equation': because of the natural log, doubling delta-v requires WAY more than double the fuel.

equations.name: Speed of light (EM wave relationship)

variables.meaning: Speed of light ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum

equations.use: Since c is fixed in vacuum, longer wavelength means lower frequency, and vice versa. Lets you convert between λ and f for any photon.

equations.name: Planck's equation
equations.use: Photon energy is proportional to frequency. Higher-frequency light (UV, X-ray, gamma) carries more energy per photon than lower-frequency light (radio, IR).

equations.name: Wien's Displacement Law
equations.use: Peak emission wavelength is inversely proportional to temperature. Hotter objects peak bluer (shorter wavelengths). The Sun (5800 K) peaks in visible; cool stars (3000 K) peak in red/IR.

equations.name: Diffraction limit (resolution)
equations.use: Sets the maximum sharpness of any telescope. Longer wavelengths or smaller apertures = blurrier images. Why radio scopes need huge dishes (or interferometric arrays) for good resolution.

equations.name: Foucault pendulum period
equations.use: Period depends only on length and gravity. Used in Lab 1 to back out the Museum of Science Boston pendulum length: L = g × (T / 2π)². For T = 8.75 s and g = 9.8, L ≈ 19.0 m.

equations.name: Drake Equation
equations.equation: N = R* × fp × ne × fl × fi × fc × L

variables.meaning: Number of communicating civilizations in the Milky Way
Fraction of stars with planets (~1, from Kepler)
Lifetime of detectable civilization (most uncertain term)

equations.use: Multiplicative estimate of the number of contactable civilizations. Earlier terms are well-constrained; biology and L terms span many orders of magnitude.

equations.name: Hubble's Law
equations.use: Galaxies recede at speeds proportional to their distance — direct evidence for an expanding universe. Used as the topmost rung of the cosmic distance ladder for very far galaxies.

equations.name: Kepler's Third Law
equations.use: Period squared is proportional to semi-major axis cubed. With G and M known: T² = (4π² / GM) × a³. Lets you compute orbital periods or distances from one another.

equations.name: Earth's rotation rate
equations.use: Earth turns 15° each hour, which is also why each time zone spans roughly 15° of longitude.

equations.name: Polaris altitude = latitude

variables.meaning: Angle of Polaris above the horizon (degrees)

equations.use: Because Polaris sits nearly on Earth's rotational axis, its angle above the horizon equals your latitude. At the North Pole, Polaris is overhead (90°); at the equator, it's on the horizon (0°).

equations.name: Sidereal vs solar day

variables.meaning: One full rotation relative to the STARS
One rotation relative to the SUN (longer because Earth also moves along its orbit)

equations.use: Explains why a star rises ~4 minutes earlier each night. The 4-minute difference per day adds up to one full extra rotation per year.

equations.name: Conservation of Energy

variables.meaning: Total energy in a closed system (joules)
m × g × h (gravitational, near Earth's surface)

equations.use: Energy transforms between forms but is never created or destroyed in a closed system. Foundational to mechanics, thermodynamics, and orbital dynamics.

equations.name: Special relativity time dilation

variables.meaning: Time interval in moving frame (proper time)
Time interval as measured from stationary frame

equations.use: Moving clocks tick slower from a stationary observer's frame. At v = 0.95c, the factor is ~3.2× — what makes interstellar travel within a human lifetime conceivable for the traveler, while Earth ages much more.

equations.name: Length contraction
equations.use: Distance to a destination shrinks for a fast-moving observer. From the astronaut's frame at 0.95c, the trip to Alpha Centauri (4.25 ly) looks like ~1.3 ly — the partner equation to time dilation.
